coredata.dc:title: Developing a fluorometric aptasensor based on carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles for the detection of adenosine
coredata.dc:creator: Yousefi, Shila
Saraji, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: A new simple fluorometric aptasensor was developed for the determination of adenosine (AD) in human urine samples. A mixture of new and green nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) as fluorescents and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) as quenchers were utilized for aptasensing. The binding of AD to anti-AD aptamer (AP) provided greater protection for unmodified AgNPs against salt-induced aggregation than AP alone. In this case, the fluorescence signal of N,S-CQDs was quenched by the dispersed AgNPs based on the inner filter effect. In the absence of AD, AgNPs tended to aggregate in a high salt solution. Hence, the fluorescence spectrum of N,S-CQDs no longer overlapped with the absorption spectrum of the aggregated AgNPs, and consequently N,S-CQDs did not quench. The linear range of the method was in the range of 50–200 nM, and the limit of detection was 13 nM. The relative standard deviations ranged from 5.2 to 7.3% (n = 6). In addition, the method had high selectivity toward AD in the presence of other interferences. The applicability of the method was examined by the quantitation of AD in urine samples of two patients through the standard addition method with recoveries in the range of 89–93%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adenosine
Aptamer
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of N,S-CQDs and metal nanoparticles
Analysis of AD in urine samples
Comparison of the method with other fluorometric methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission spectra of N,S-CQDs in the presence of (a) AgNPs and (b) AuNPs. (1) Without salt addition, (2) addition of AP, AD, and NaCl at the concentration of 8.3 nM, 160 nM, and 33.3 mM, respectively, (3) addition of AP and NaCl at the concentration of 8.3 nM and 33.3 mM, respectively, and (4) addition of 33.3 mM NaCl.
Emission spectra and the linear relationship of fluorescence intensity of (a) AgNPs-AP-NaCl and (b) AuNPs-AP-NaCl systems in the presence of different concentrations of AD.

The response of the sensor in the presence of guanine, thymine, urea, l-lysine, dl-methionine, l-threonine, AD and mixture of all compounds. The concentration of interfering compounds and AD were 500 and 160 nM, respectively. Blank solution was pure water. (All measurements were repeated six times).
The intra-day and inter-day precisions for the determination of AD.
Summary of results obtained for 450-fold diluted real sample analysis.
The comparison of the proposed method with the other fluorometric techniques used for the determination of AD in urine samples.

row.entry: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between CdS QDs as a donor and polypyrrole as an acceptor
The fluorescence of AP-modified QDs was quenched by polypyrrole. In the presence of AD, the fluorescence of QDs was returned, and its intensity was proportional to the AD concentration.
Silver ions-assisted cation exchange reaction with CdTe QDs

Two pieces of ssDNA were attached to magnetic nanoparticles and QDs. AD was reassembled the ssDNA and formed the sandwich structure. After magnetic separation, the fluorescence signal based on silver ions-assisted twice cation exchange reactions would detect AD.
Label-free fluorescence dual-amplified detection based on exonuclease III-assisted DNA cycling and hybridization chain reaction

The catalytic strand induced the Exo III-assisted DNA cycling reaction and produced a large amount of DNA fragments. AD was converted to a large amount of DNA triggers, leading to a significant amplification.
A gold nanoparticles-modified AP beacon based on structure switching/fluorescence-turning on mechanism
The AP was designed into an AP beacon by adding AuNPs-modified DNA, which was complementary to AP sequence. The fluorescence signal was turning on when AP beacon was bound to AD.
Fluorescence enhancement using AP–gold nanoparticles, and a fluorescent DNA-binding dye
The presence of AD caused the less binding of AP onto the surface of AuNPs, resulting in the fluorescence change of the fluorescent DNA.
Interaction of dispersive/aggregative AgNPs with green N,S-CQDs
The AP bound with AD and protected AgNPs to prevent their aggregation in the high salt solution. As a result, the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs was quenched by dispersive AgNPs.

head.ce:title: Developing a fluorometric aptasensor based on carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles for the detection of adenosine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new simple fluorometric aptasensor was developed for the determination of adenosine (AD) in human urine samples. A mixture of new and green nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) as fluorescents and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) as quenchers were utilized for aptasensing. The binding of AD to anti-AD aptamer (AP) provided greater protection for unmodified AgNPs against salt-induced aggregation than AP alone. In this case, the fluorescence signal of N,S-CQDs was quenched by the dispersed AgNPs based on the inner filter effect. In the absence of AD, AgNPs tended to aggregate in a high salt solution. Hence, the fluorescence spectrum of N,S-CQDs no longer overlapped with the absorption spectrum of the aggregated AgNPs, and consequently N,S-CQDs did not quench. The linear range of the method was in the range of 50–200 nM, and the limit of detection was 13 nM. The relative standard deviations ranged from 5.2 to 7.3% (n = 6). In addition, the method had high selectivity toward AD in the presence of other interferences. The applicability of the method was examined by the quantitation of AD in urine samples of two patients through the standard addition method with recoveries in the range of 89–93%.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have attracted much attention in fluorometric-based analytical methods, due to their excellent extinction coefficient and distance-dependent optical properties, easy preparation, and high biocompatibility [1]. These metal-nanomaterials with broad UV–vis absorption bands can quench the fluorescence of many fluorophores [2–4]. This is because of the high overlap integrals of their absorption bands with the fluorescence spectra of fluorophores [3]. In some cases, the fluorescence of fluorophores can be turned on by the addition of an analyte with a particular functional group (e.g., thiolated analytes).

Today, aptamers (APs) have attracted extensive attention because they can selectively recognize various targets. They bind to their targets and go through a conformational structural change [5–9]. In earlier fluorescence aptasensors, amino-functionalized-AP modified fluorophores have been used as fluorescence probes, where their fluorescence was turned off by a quencher [5]. When the quencher was replaced by the analyte, the fluorescence of the fluorophore was restored. The fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore was proportional to analyte concentration. Despite the high sensitivity and selectivity of these aptasensors, preparing modified-fluorophores was a time-consuming, centrifuge-dependent and non-economical process. In 2016, Wang et al. [2] modified the surface of AuNPs by a thiolated-AP and used them as quenchers. Although this modification was less time-consuming than the previous method, it was only applicable with AuNPs [1]. For other metal nanoparticles such as AgNPs, the long modification time lead to the oxidation of AgNPs [1]. In a recent study, an unfunctionalized-AP as the recognition probe, quantum dots (QDs) as fluorophores and AuNPs as quenchers have been applied for detecting adenosine 5′-triphosphate [10]. The method was straightforward and fast and did not require a time-consuming process. Adding salt triggered electrostatic attraction between negatively charged AuNPs, resulting in the aggregation of the metal nanoparticles. The negatively charged unfunctionalized-AP gave partial dispersity to AuNPs, and the absorption spectrum of the aggregated metal nanoparticles no longer overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of QDs. As a result, the fluorescence of QDs did not quench. In the presence of the analyte, AP selectively bound to it and formed an AP-analyte complex. The metal nanoparticles were more dispersed in the presence of the AP-analyte complex (folded AP) than the AP alone (unfolded AP) in a high salt solution. Thus, the dispersed metal-nanomaterials quenched the fluorescence emission of QDs. Analyte quantification was based on the fluorescence quenching of QDs, which was directly related to the dispersion degree of the metal nanoparticles [10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanoparticles with a size below 10 nm. CQDs have superior properties including low toxicity, favorable photostability, low-cost, good water-solubility, environmentally friendly and facile synthesis. They have been used in various fields including drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, chemical sensing and catalysis [11–16]. Due to the low quantum yield (QY) and easy oxidization of CQDs [10,11], they are usually modified to improve their photoluminescence intensity and chemical stability [11]. The modification usually includes capping the raw CQDs with an organic polymer or an inorganic salt, oxidation of the surface of CQDs with strong acids, and doping CQDs with heteroatoms (sulfur or nitrogen). Chemical modification through heteroatom doping is preferred due to its simplicity and rapidity [17–20]. Moreover, co-doping with N and S encourages the electron transfer rate [18]. L-cysteine and thiourea as precursors of both nitrogen and sulfur have been utilized for doping CQDs (N,S-CQDs) [18–20]. Modification by l-cysteine is performed through a long-term hydrothermal method (>12 h) [18,19], and the obtained N,S-CQDs from thiourea has a relatively low QY (26.9%) [20].

Determination of adenosine (AD) in urine samples has received great attention in analytical and biomedical studies [21]. Some studies have reported that AD can be pronounced as a possible cancer biomarker [22,23]. Several techniques have been employed to detect AD, but recently, with the evolution of biosensors, fluorometric aptasensors with high sensitivity and selectivity for AD detection have attracted much attention [5–9]. However, the previous aptasensors needed toxic heavy metals for the preparation of QDs [5,6], and complicated time-consuming procedures for synthesizing QDs/quenchers and immobilization of AP [5–9].

The measuring principle of the present work has previously been reported [10]. However, the previous work mostly focused on the synthesis and properties of terbium ion-coordinated CQDs and using AuNPs as quenchers. Although, the synthesized CQDs had high photoluminescence QY, the two-step, and long synthesis process, and using three precursor materials were the disadvantages of the previous work. In the present work, for the first time, citrate-caped AgNPs were used as eco-friendly aptasensor quenchers for the fluorometric detection of AD. N,S-CQDs as fluorophores were synthesized through a green one-step hydrothermal procedure. DL-methionine was used as the nitrogen and sulfur source for modifying CQDs. Unfunctionalized-AP was applied as the recognition probe. The formed AP-AD complex prevented the aggregation of AgNPs in a high salt solution. Thus, the dispersed AgNPs quenched the fluorescence emission of N,S-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing AD concentration. The linear range, sensitivity, and repeatability of the method were appraised. AuNPs as commonly used metal nanoparticles applied in aptasensors were also evaluated as quenchers, and the results were compared with those obtained using AgNPs. Finally, the method was applied to determine AD in urine samples of lung cancer patients.

All the reagents were at least of analytical reagent grade unless specified. Pure water was prepared by a system consisted of ion exchange and carbon cartridges. The synthetic anti-AD AP (5′-ACC TGG GGG AGT ATT GCG GAG GAA GGT-3′) was obtained from Macrogen (Seoul, South Korea). Urea, sodium chloride, ascorbic acid, citric acid, chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), silver nitrate, trisodium citrate dihydrate and tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Adenosine, thymine, guanine, l-lysine, dl-methionine, l-threonine and quinine sulfate were obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). A Lambda 25, PerkinElmer UV–vis spectrophotometer (Boston, USA) was used for absorbance measurements. Fluorescence spectra were measured by an RF-5301PC-Shimadzu fluorescence spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with Xe-900 lamp (Edinburgh Instrument, UK).

The synthesized N,S-CQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (350, Jasco Co., Tokyo, Japan). Elemental analysis was performed by a 932 CHNS elemental analyzer from LECO (St Joseph, MI, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained with a Philips CM30 (Eindhoven, The Netherlands) at an accelerating voltage of 150 kV. An SZ-100 laser particle size analyzer (Horiba Scientific, Kyoto, Japan) was used to record the dynamic light scattering (DLS) histograms and zeta potentials. The data were evaluated by means of a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) using Microsoft Excel 2016.

The details of AuNPs and AgNPs synthesis were described in Supporting information. AuNPs were prepared by the reduction of a HAuCl4 solution according to a previous report [24]. AgNPs were synthesized via the chemical reduction of AgNO3 using citrate and ascorbic acid [25].

N,S-CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal treatment as follows: 2.3 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of dl-methionine were dissolved in 15 mL of pure water and then transferred into a 25-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C in a period of 6 h. After that, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The resulting mixture was dispersed in 15 mL of pure water and sonicated for 30 min. After removing the large dots by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 20 min, N,S-CQDs solution was diluted with pure water until the final concentration of 2 mg mL−1 was obtained. The suspension was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further characterization and use. The concentration of N,S-CQDs was determined by taking 2 mL of the undiluted suspension and weighing after drying in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

The QY of N,S-CQDs was determined by quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54) as a standard compound by the following equation [26]:
Φ =
Φ std
Grad x
Grad std
η x
η std
2

Φ is the QY, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent (η
x =η
std ), and A refers to the absorbance which should be <0.1 at the excitation wavelength to minimize reabsorption effects. The subscript std refers to the standard fluorophore with known QY (quinine sulfate), and x refers to N,S-CQDs solution.

To evaluate the potential of the new aptasensor in real sample analysis, the detection of AD was done in fresh urine samples of two lung cancer patients. Samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min; the supernatants were filtered through a nylon filter (0.45 μm) and diluted 15-fold with pure water. 100 μL of the diluted solution was used to quantify the analyte by the standard addition method. Considering the final volume of the solution in the detection cell (i.e., 3 mL), the samples were totally diluted 450-fold.

At first, 100 μL of AD solution with different concentrations and 100 μL of anti-AD AP (250 nM) were added to 200 μL of a buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM Tris–HCl and 20 mM KCl). The mixture was shaken completely and incubated for 15 min at room temperature. After that, 2 mL of AgNPs or AuNPs colloidal was added to the suspension mixture and left for 10 min. The final concentrations of AgNPs and AuNPs were 0.67 and 6.7 nM, respectively. Then, 200 μL of NaCl solution (500 mM) was added into the mixture to induce the prompt aggregation of the nanomaterials. Finally, 50 μL of N,S-CQDs (final concentration of 33 μg mL−1) was added to the mixture. Then the mixture was diluted to 3 mL with pure water. The fluorescence measurement was done at λex = 340 nm and λem = 440 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the blank to the fluorescence intensity of AD (F0/F) was measured against AD concentration. All measurements were performed six times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N,S-CQDs and metal nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 (Supporting information) depicts the FTIR spectrum of N,S-CQDs. The peak at 3300 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of NH and OH [17]. The characteristic bands at 2900 and 2343 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of CH and SH, respectively [17]. The strong band around 1668 cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of –CO-NH [27]. The absorption peaks at 1545, 1458, 1401 and 1245 cm−1 indicated the presence of NH, COO−, CN and CO bands, respectively. The absorption bands between 1140 and 1000 cm−1 were attributed to -SO3 −, C-O-C and CN bonds [17,27].
Elemental analysis data indicated that N,S-CQDs were mainly composed of carbon (50.1%), nitrogen (4.9%), sulfur (5.5%) and hydrogen (1.3%).

Fig. 1 illustrates the morphology and size characteristics of the prepared N,S-CQDs. The TEM image revealed that N,S-CQDs had a uniform size and spherical structure. Both TEM and DLS results confirmed that the diameter of N,S-CQDs were lower than 10 nm, and the average particle size was found to be 4.7 ± 1.2 nm (Fig. S2, Supporting information). The zeta potential of the N,S-CQDs was −19.2 ± 1.0 mV, which confirms the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of N,S-CQDs [28]. This high zeta potential value supports the aqueous solubility and stability of N,S-CQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDs (Fig. S3, Supporting information) showed two absorption peaks around 250 and 340 nm. The peak at 250 nm was mainly attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC bonds, and the other peak at 340 nm was ascribed to the n-π* transitions of heteroatom (N, O or S)-containing bonds [29].

The fluorescence performance of N,S-CQDs was checked at several excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S4a (Supporting information), the maximum emission wavelength shifted to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increased from 290 to 360 nm. The wavelength of maximum fluorescence emission was between 430 and 450 nm when N,S-CQDs were excited at 340 nm (Fig. S4, Supporting information). The shoulder around 370 nm in the fluorescence spectrum suggested that the emissions are associated with two different types of electronically excited states in the heterogeneous electronic structure of the N,S-CQDs [30]. The QY of 65% was calculated at the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths (340/440). The details of quantum yield measurement were explained in Supporting information (Fig. S5, Supporting information). The existence of nitrogen and sulfur atoms in N,S-CQDs made the material an excellent electron donor with high QY [18,19].

The size and shape of AgNPs and AuNPs were characterized by TEM and DLS (Fig. S6, Supporting information). The size of AuNPs and AgNPs was about 13.8 ± 3.8 nm and 14.1 ± 3.9 nm, respectively. The zeta potentials of AuNPs and AgNPs were determined to be −35.1 ± 2.2 mV and − 30.7 ± 1.7 mV, respectively.

The citrate-modified AuNPs and AgNPs dispersed well in aqueous solutions due to their electrostatic repulsion of negative charges, preventing strong van der Waals attraction between the metal nanoparticles. After adding salt (NaCl), the metal nanoparticles aggregated due to neutralizing their negative charges. In the presence of low amount of AP, the coordination complex of AP-metal nanoparticles was formed, and the electrostatic repulsion of negative phosphate groups of AP partially prevented the aggregation of the metal nanoparticles. The main interaction between AP and metal nanoparticles is van der Waals forces between nitrogen atoms of various bases of AP with metal nanoparticles [31–33]. This interaction is stronger than the electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphate backbone and metal nanoparticles [1]. The different dispersity of AP-metal nanoparticles complex in the presence of AD (folded AP) and absence of AD (unfolded AP) in the high salt solution was the basis of analyte detection (Fig. 2 ). The AP-AD complex formed a structure, in which interaction sites of AP were more available to interact with the metal nanoparticles. As a result, AP-AD complex could keep the dispersity of metal nanoparticles more significantly than the AP alone in the high salt solution [10,34,35].

The emission intensity of N,S-CQDs decreased by the addition of the metal nanoparticles (75–80% quenching efficiency, as seen in Fig. 2, curve 1). Considering the significant overlap of fluorescence spectrum of N,S-CQDs and the absorption bands of the metal nanoparticles (Fig. S7, Supporting information), the quenching of N,S-CQDs by the metal nanoparticles may be induced by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or the inner filter effect (IFE) [36]. The metal nanoparticles and N,S-CQDs possess negative charges as confirmed by the zeta potential values (Section 3.1). The FRET process would involve the intermolecular connection of N,S-CQDs with the metal nanoparticles at a particular distance or geometry to enable the interaction between them. Therefore, the assembly of these FRET-based sensors is usually a complicated chemical process involving the modification of the metal-nanoparticles or N,S-CQDs surface. As a result, there was no interaction between the metal nanoparticles and N,S-CQDs. The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CQDs in the aqueous solution was 15.1 ± 0.7 ns. In addition, the lifetime of N,S-CQDs was 14.8 ± 0.8 ns and 15.0 ± 0.7 ns with AuNPs and AgNPs, respectively. Almost no lifetime change of N,S-CQDs was observed, which indicated that there was no significant interaction between N,S-CQDs and the metal nanoparticles in their excited-states. It demonstrated that the fluorescence change of N,S-CQDs by the metal nanoparticles resulted from the simple absorption of the excitation and emission light by the absorber (IFE mechanism) [37]. Since the absorption spectra of the metal nanoparticles remained unchanged in the presence of N,S-CQDs (Fig. S8, Supporting information), no complex formation and energy transfer between N,S-CQDs and the metal nanoparticles occurred. Moreover, the similarity of fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs (without the metal nanoparticles) and the mixture of N,S-CQDs and AD (Fig. S9, Supporting information) indicated that there was no interaction between AD and N,S-CQDs. Furthermore, quenching did not happen in the absence of the metal nanoparticles. These phenomena supported the significant quench of N,S-CQDs by the metal nanoparticles via the IFE [37–41]. In a high salt solution, in the presence of AP, the IFE efficiency was decreased and the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs was recovered. In the presence of AD, the formed complex between AD and AP avoided the aggregation of metal nanoparticles. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs significantly reduced (over 60–70%) compared to that in the absence of AD with a concentration of 160 nM (Scheme 1 and Fig. 2). The obvious red-shift of the emission peak in the presence of AD (Fig. 2a) was a result of partial overlapping of the absorption spectrum of AgNPs and the emission spectrum of N,S-CQDs (Fig. 7S, Supporting information) [42,43].

Calibration curves of the method for the quantification of AD were obtained using the fluorescence signal of the AD solution at various concentrations. The changes in the fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQDs indicated that the aggregation of metal nanoparticles gradually reduced with increasing the concentration of AD (Fig. 3 ). The fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing AD concentration. The calibration curves of fluorescence intensity (F0/F) against AD concentration are shown in Fig. 3. The linear concentration ranges from 25 to 180 nM, and 50 to 200 nM were obtained using AuNPs and AgNPs, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated through 3S
b /m, where S
b is the standard deviation of blank (n = 10), and m is the slope of the calibration curve. The LODs were 8.4 and 13 nM using AuNPs and AgNPs, respectively.

The intra- and inter-day relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the method for the determination of AD were calculated. To investigate the repeatability of the proposed method, six repeated measurements were performed at the concentration levels of 60, 100 and 150 nM (Table 1 ). The intra-day RSDs were in the range of 5.2–6.8% for AgNPs and 4.0–4.7% for AuNPs. The inter-day precisions were 5.5–7.3% for AgNPs and 5.2–6.3% for AuNPs. The stability of the sensors was investigated by measuring their response in the solution of 160 nM AD within 60 min. The responses decreased only about 5% (AgNPs) and 4% (AuNPs) under continuous UV light illumination after 1 h. The sensors were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator when not in use. After 48 h, the response of the sensors was reduced by ~9% (AgNPs) and ~7% (AuNPs). The results showed that the sensors had a relativity good storage stability.

To evaluate the selectivity of the aptasensors for detecting AD, the response of the sensors for non-target molecules (guanine, thymine, urea, l-lysine, dl-methionine, l-threonine) at the concentration of 500 nM was measured. A solution containing all of the studied compounds and AD at 160 nM was also analyzed by the method. All measurements were done six times. Guanine has a structure somewhat similar to AD. Thymine was selected because it has amino functional groups similar to the analyte [5]. Urea is found in urine samples [6], and amino acids as diagnostic biomarkers exist in the urine of cancer patients [44]. Regarding the results shown in Fig. 4 , there was no significant difference between the response of the blank and the non-target molecules using AuNPs and AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AD in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature [7], the concentration of AD in the urine sample of a normal human (from 2.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 to 7.0 × 10−6 mol L−1) is significantly lower than that of cancer patients (5–10 order less). Regarding the LOD of the method, detection of AD in the urine sample of a normal human is possible.

To demonstrate the practical application of the method for determining AD as the cancer biomarker, the concentration of AD was determined in the urine of two lung cancer patients. The samples were prepared and analyzed according to the procedure explained in the experimental section. The samples were spiked with AD at two concentration levels (20 and 50 nM), and recoveries were calculated. The recovery of AD was in the range of 89–93% with RSDs from 4.7 to 7.5% (Table 2 ). The results confirmed the adequate precision and accuracy of the method for urine analysis. Furthermore, the results of real sample analysis obtained by AgNPs and AuNPs were statistically compared with a standard method (HPLC–UV) [45] using one-way ANOVA. As shown in Table S1 (Supporting information), all P-values were higher than 0.05. It demonstrated that there was no statistically significant difference between the present method and the standard method. However, the present method was more precise than the standard method. In addition, the standard method was considerably more laborious (concerning the preparation of a sample and analysis time) than the developed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the method with other fluorometric methods
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the present method, its analytical performance was compared with that of current fluorescence-based techniques used for AD determination [5–9]. Most of the current methods suffer from complicated and long-term steps, and using toxic raw materials (Table 3 ). In previously reported methods, a relatively long time in the range of 4–32 h is required for AP immobilization. However, the whole immobilization and analysis time in the present method was 25 min. The LOD and RSDs of the present method are comparable with those of previous reports.

In summary, we have successfully introduced a sensitive and selective fluorescence aptasensor for the fast determination of AD. For developing the fluorescence sensor, N,S-CQDs were synthesized from low-price materials through a short-time, green and one-step hydrothermal procedure. N,S-CQDs showed high QY due to the presence of sulfur and nitrogen atoms in their structure. Compared to the most of the previously reported fluorescence aptasensors for detecting AD which used toxic heavy metals for preparing QDs, a complicated synthesis procedure for preparing quenchers, and long-term immobilization processes, the new AgNPs aptasensor was a time-saving, simple, sensitive, specific, and cost-effective technique. The present method showed successful results for quantifying AD in urine samples of lung cancer patients. Compared to AuNPs which are normally applied as quenchers in aptasensing detection, AgNPs as eco-friendly materials showed more selectivity toward the analyte.
This work was supported by the Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) and the Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2019.04.083.
